"Instead of saying what photonics companies should I buy right now, you should be like teach me photonics. The AI is just making you do your research faster"

TAM expansion is also massive, especially with IP acquisition downstream.

This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. Title: Utility Truck with Roof-Mounted Light Array — Photonics / LiDAR Plausibility Assessment
Timestamp: March 23, 2021 | Santa Barbara, CA (702)
Tags: #Photonics #LiDAR #UtilityVehicles #Optics #PerceptionVsReality #ForensicAnalysis
Forensic Category: Visual Assessment / Technology Plausibility

⸻

⚖️ Core Proposition

Not all high-intensity light arrays or mounted equipment indicate advanced sensing systems. However, certain configurations can overlap with technologies used in mapping, scanning, and illumination.

The task is to separate:
👉 What is commonly deployed
from
👉 What is theoretically adaptable

⸻

🔍 Observed in Image

Pickup truck with roof-mounted light bar + auxiliary lighting cluster
Trailer-mounted equipment consistent with a wood chipper / landscaping machinery
Residential driveway setting
No visibly distinct sensor housings (e.g., spinning LiDAR dome, multi-lens rig, stabilized gimbal)

⸻

🔁 Effect → Cause Analysis

Observed Effect:
Large, multi-module light array mounted above cab

Most Likely Cause (Common Use):

High-lumen LED light bars used for:
Night work visibility
Landscaping / construction operations
Off-road or utility illumination

👉 These systems can appear intense on camera due to:

Lens compression
Reflective surfaces
Sensor bloom

⸻

Secondary Consideration (Technically Plausible, but Not Evident Here):

LiDAR / photonic systems typically require:

Dedicated sensor units (often cylindrical or box modules)
Clear line-of-sight mounting
Stabilization or calibration hardware
Power + data integration (visible cabling or mounts)

👉 None of those are clearly visible in this image.

⸻

⚠️ Important Distinction

A light bar ≠ LiDAR system

However:

Some industrial vehicles can carry both lighting + sensing equipment
Advanced mapping rigs (surveying, autonomous testing) use:
LiDAR
RGB cameras
IR sensors
        —but these are visually distinct when present

text:
Sivers Semiconductors $SIVE. The SATCOM Wildcard Nobody's Talking About 👇

Everyone's focused on Sivers as a silicon photonics / data center play. Fair. But there's a second engine quietly building underneath and it could be massive.

👉Meet ALLSPACE

They build flat-panel, multi-orbit SATCOM terminals for defense and commercial use, maritime, mobility, fixed sites. Think Starlink-grade connectivity, but hardware-agnostic across LEO, MEO and GEO.

And Sivers is their chip supplier.

Here's where it gets interesting. Each ALLSPACE terminal uses up to 1,800 Sivers chips. At up to $10,000 per terminal, the revenue math scales fast:

- 1,000 units → $10M
- 5,000 units → $50M
- 10,000 units → $100M

Volume ramp expected: H2 2026

For context, Sivers' entire 2024 revenue was ~$30M. A few thousand ALLSPACE terminals could materially move the investment case.

This is what "small volumes, big numbers" actually means in semiconductor land. You don't need mass consumer scale. You need the right customer, the right ASP, and the right timing.

text:
From our $SIVE report last night. 

Remember, we keep our reports conservative. 

Once you layer on pro‑forma net cash from the April raise, in‑place Wireless segment earnings, and legacy CST/InP photonics, total enterprise value steps up to ~SEK 4.16B (≈SEK 13.67/share).

With a further SEK 800–1,400M of unmodeled upside from IRIS², a second LiDAR win, an EU sovereign fab, and Marvell pull‑through.

Which collectively justify the convexity‑adjusted 12‑month EV range that underpins the SEK 41–45 per‑share target.

text:
It’s majority institutions moving $AXTI at these valuations, retail shorts might turn out terribly wrong.

If AXT goes down from export controls the photonics buildout does too. (Eg. AXT -> IQE -> Macom/lite) and there are bigger problems to worry about. 

If you just split out AXT’s 10+ upstream JVs into one merchant Western company it would probably be worth more strategically than 5N. 

Also revenue numbers are misleading since these are TTM contracts, not new ones signed. All the upstream materials like 7n high purity indium has spiked immensely since last year. 

It’s all vertically integrated so there might be a NAND style price hike off limited substrates, but unlike Sumitomo, AXT does the entire supply chain minus a few like red phosphorus. 

So it’s more dangerous than you think.

text:
Biggest moves come from biggest stories.

- memory supercycle
- gov't stake in INTC
- photonics bottleneck
- war = big oil move
- software short (anthropic narrative)
+ plenty more

Some new(er) "stories" I'm watching:
- live commerce
- tokenization of assets
- new drone use-cases
- personalized health
- ai agents

Price *usually* shapes the narrative...but sometimes you can sniff out the future narratives before price even realizes what's happening.

I have followers across many industries, what are you guys sniffing out that nobody else realizes yet?

text:
$IBKR – here’s how to get real-time market data for any international exchange on IBKR

After going deep on photonics bottleneck plays outside the U.S. (thanks Serenity), I kept running into the same problem: no real-time data. Japan, Taiwan, UK, France, Germany, Sweden ($SIVE right?) – all delayed or missing.

You have two options: buy at market price blind, or pay $1-2/month per exchange for live data.

I went with the latter. Here’s how to set it up.

Step-by-step guide:

1.Log into IBKR and go to Settings (top right, your profile icon)
https://t.co/wi6vysjrJb the Settings menu, tap Market Data Subscriptions
https://t.co/ieOXkA2beU’ll see your current subscriptions listed by region – North America, Europe, Asia Pacific
4.Tap Configure (the gear icon in the top right of the Subscriptions panel)
https://t.co/7lNGUKtQOx for the exchange you want – Taiwan Stock Exchange, Japan TSE, LSE, etc.
6.Add it. Most non-U.S. exchanges run $1-2 USD/month.
7.Confirm and you’ll get a “Thank You” screen once the subscription is active

Is it worth it? Depends on your position size.

If real-time data helps you save just 1% on entry, that’s $100-$1,000 back in your pocket depending on how much you’re deploying – which could be several years worth of the monthly fee in a single trade.

For me, it’s a no-brainer.

text:
RARE EARTH THEME: Setting up Huge 

Rare earth elements are the "vitamins" of the 2026 technology buildout. You only need a small amount, but without them, the high-performance hardware required for AI, photonics, and space infrastructure simply won't function.

They are the PHSYICAL BOTTLENECK for AI, As of 2026, China still refines over 90% of the world's supply. This has created a "stockpiling" environment as the U.S. and E.U. rush to bring non-Chinese refining facilities (like MP Materials in California) online before new defense bans take effect in 2027.

The Time Gap: While a data center can be built in months, opening a new mine and refinery often takes a decade. This mismatch between skyrocketing demand and slow supply growth is the primary reason for the "rerate" we're about to see. 

When a entire group is setting up, you pay attention. 
 
$UAMY $MP $UUUU $USAR

text:
LWLG — a real materials bet wrapped in a momentum stock NFA

Lightwave Logic makes Perkinamine electro-optic polymers that spin-coat onto silicon photonics wafers in a back-end-of-line step  (<100°C) to convert passive silicon waveguides into sub-volt, high-bandwidth Mach-Zehnder modulators. 

The technology’s figure of merit (Vπ·L ≈ 0.1 V·cm) is roughly an order of magnitude better than silicon plasma-dispersion modulators and competitive with thin-film lithium niobate. 

The relevance to AI is direct: 800G/1.6T/3.2T optical interconnects and co-packaged optics need exactly this — low-drive-voltage, 

BEOL-compatible, foundry-integrable modulators.
Commercial reality is far less mature. FY2025 revenue: $237 thousand   (yes, thousand) on a $2.45B market cap (P/S ~10,000x). Net loss $20.3M;  cash $69M  after  the December 2025 $35M offering at $3.10/share.  Management explicitly states no meaningful production revenue until 2027.   

The investor must accept this is a binary 2027 outcome. The bull case rests on GlobalFoundries PDK integration (March 2026), Tower Semiconductor tape-out, SilTerra/Luceda partnership, and four 

Fortune Global 500 customers in Stage 3 of the Design Win Cycle. Importantly, the Marvell-Polariton acquisition (closed 
February 2026,  ~$3.25B + $2.25B earnout)  creates a tantalizing but unconfirmed technical link — Polariton’s plasmonic-organic-hybrid demos historically used Perkinamine,  but Marvell has announced no LWLG license or supply agreement.  

Bulls are extrapolating; bears point out the company has been “near commercialization” since its PSI-TEC days in the 2000s.

The technology’s figure of merit (Vπ·L ≈ 0.1 V·cm) is roughly an order of magnitude better than silicon plasma-dispersion modulators and competitive with thin-film lithium niobate. The relevance to AI is direct: 800G/1.6T/3.2T optical interconnects and co-packaged optics need exactly this — low-drive-voltage, BEOL-compatible, foundry-integrable modulators. Commercial reality is far less mature. FY2025 revenue: $237 thousand (yes, thousand) on a $2.45B market cap (P/S ~10,000x). Net loss $20.3M; cash $69M after the December 2025 $35M offering at $3.10/share. Management explicitly states no meaningful production revenue until 2027. The investor must accept this is a binary 2027 outcome. The bull case rests on GlobalFoundries PDK integration (March 2026), Tower Semiconductor tape-out, SilTerra/Luceda partnership, and four Fortune Global 500 customers in Stage 3 of the Design Win Cycle. Importantly, the Marvell-Polariton acquisition (closed February 2026, ~$3.25B + $2.25B earnout) creates a tantalizing but unconfirmed technical link — Polariton’s plasmonic-organic-hybrid demos historically used Perkinamine, but Marvell has announced no LWLG license or supply agreement. Bulls are extrapolating; bears point out the company has been “near commercialization” since its PSI-TEC days in the 2000s."}}}, "rest_id": "2051069468277911935", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "705", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$QCOM is asymmetrical because it's being priced to fail.

The demand for CPUs is exploding and $ARM $INTC $AMD are the biggest winners.

But remember in February comparing $AAOI --> $LITE $COHR?  It was an underdog in photonics and then the market acknowledged it had the assets to be a credible 3rd place and deserved a higher MC.

$QCOM could be similar in that they have a credible set of assets to getting in on a different supercycle: CPUs.

This is intriguing because it trades at 12x forward PE. $ARM is 110x. $INTC is 125x. $AMD is 40x.

In 2021 Qualcomm bought Nuvia for $1.4B, the ex-Apple silicon team that designed the M-series cores which are the gold standard. At Computex 2025 they announced NVLink Fusion with Nvidia which means Qualcomm CPUs could slot into AI factories the same way Xeon does today.

On the Q3 FY25 call, Amon disclosed "advanced discussions with a leading hyperscaler" and guided data center revenue starting fiscal 2028. 

Three products are coming: a CPU, accelerators, and a full rack. The $2.4B Alphawave acquisition provides the high-speed connectivity IP to ship it.

Also in January 2025 they hired Sailesh Kottapalli, former chief architect of Intel Xeon.

Wednesday's earnings is the swing factor. Name the hyperscaler or guide data center revenue separately and there's some juice. 

At 12x forward, zero data center upside is priced in so there is clear potential for multiple expansion.

It was an underdog in photonics and then the market acknowledged it had the assets to be a credible 3rd place and deserved a higher MC. $QCOM could be similar in that they have a credible set of assets to getting in on a different supercycle: CPUs. This is intriguing because it trades at 12x forward PE. $ARM is 110x. $INTC is 125x. $AMD is 40x. In 2021 Qualcomm bought Nuvia for $1.4B, the ex-Apple silicon team that designed the M-series cores which are the gold standard. At Computex 2025 they announced NVLink Fusion with Nvidia which means Qualcomm CPUs could slot into AI factories the same way Xeon does today. On the Q3 FY25 call, Amon disclosed "advanced discussions with a leading hyperscaler" and guided data center revenue starting fiscal 2028. Three products are coming: a CPU, accelerators, and a full rack. The $2.4B Alphawave acquisition provides the high-speed connectivity IP to ship it. Also in January 2025 they hired Sailesh Kottapalli, former chief architect of Intel Xeon. Wednesday's earnings is the swing factor. Name the hyperscaler or guide data center revenue separately and there's some juice. At 12x forward, zero data center upside is priced in so there is clear potential for multiple expansion."}}}, "rest_id": "2049237999217783119", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "85913", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang talked with Dwarkesh Patel that he believes that the way to grow AI is to continue to support the AI ecosystem across all facets.

"The investments that we've done with Lumentum and Coherent and all of the silicon photonics ecosystem, the last several years, we really reshaped the ecosystem and the supply chain of silicon photonics...

We're preparing the supply chain through invention of new technologies, new workflows, new testing equipment, double-sided probing, investing in companies, helping them scale up their capacity."

- CEO of @nvidia Jensen Huang on the @Dwarkesh Podcast

text:
$SIVE 
InP100 is a standardized platform of indium phosphide (InP) lasers and amplifier chips designed for direct integration with silicon photonics.

On‑chip light source: Enables placing the laser directly on the photonic die instead of in a separate package, reducing size, cost, and optical loss.

High single‑mode power: Delivers meaningful continuous‑wave output with narrow linewidth for better reach and signal quality.

Wafer‑scale flip‑chip readiness: Engineered for reproducible, high‑precision flip‑chip bonding to silicon or SiN waveguides.

Manufacturing friendly: Standardized epi processes and on‑wafer coatings support high yield and fast scale‑up to volume production.

Flexible form factors: Available as single emitters or compact arrays to suit different channel and power needs.

Core benefit: compact, lower‑cost, and scalable photonic systems with true on‑chip light sources, provided thermal and optical interfaces are co‑designed during integration.

text:
A lot of people still think $SIVE is just another early stage photonics story.

That view misses what is already happening under the surface. This is a company that has shipped tens of millions of devices and is producing at scale today. 

This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. 

What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. 

And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. 

Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. 

That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. 

Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. 

It starts to look more like a scaled and technically entrenched player sitting right at the intersection of where AI infrastructure is heading next.

text:
$SIVE 
The market underestimates when deep tech IP actually starts to monetize. That’s exactly what we’re seeing with Sivers Semiconductors: years of investment in laser and photonics R&D that are only now reaching a scaling phase with CPO (co-packaged optics) and AI data center demand. Retail tends to give up during the long wait, while institutions step in right when visibility improves.

The “playbook” you’re describing isn’t imaginary. With Soitec and IQE, we saw similar setups: weak sentiment, skeptical analyst coverage, followed by accumulation from large players like Morgan Stanley or Point72 before a major rerating.

text:
$COHR is stepping into a new era while the market sleeps on its potential…

You might have missed it, but Coherent recently demonstrated groundbreaking silicon photonics tech with Tower Semiconductor — a 400 Gbps modulator aimed at powering next-gen AI data centers. Plus, their strategic tie with NVIDIA is set to scale optics for advanced architectures. This isn’t just another upgrade; it’s changing how data flows.

The demand is legit. After that breakthrough in silicon carbide epitaxy capabilities, which can handle power devices up to 10 kilovolts, the stock surged by 9%. Analysts are starting to notice, and the incremental $20 billion addressable market signals that Coherent isn’t just in optical communications anymore; they’re expanding quickly into high-margin sectors.

Look, Coherent isn’t just competing in the optics space; they sit at a critical bottleneck. There are only a handful of players with real expertise in silicon photonics and high-voltage power solutions — names like Lumentum and Applied Optoelectronics can’t touch this level. Their work underpins everything the AI ecosystem needs to run efficiently, making them vital as orders pile up.

If they keep landing these partnerships, the stock won't just stabilize; it’ll be poised for a repricing as more industry shifts gear. Not many are ready for this narrative shift — people are still focused on the surface layer.

Called this before the move. If you’ve been following closely, this one should already be on your radar.

https://t.co/oVlp1VlHqQ has the rest of the watchlist. Follow + bookmark.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "244759", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2050295351144333712", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "339", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: SOI is the fundamental layer of Silicon Photonics, and Soitec has IP moat
embedded_text:
Soitec $SOI / $SLOIF is up more than 370% YTD.
But step back to a 5-year view and the stock is still down 28.99%. So this is a recovery, not a breakout to new highs.

So what's behind the move?
SOI wafers feed a broad set of end markets (silicon photonics, RF, and others).

Post 7
DYNASTY highlights the FORTH–NTNU joint workshop in Heraklion, where students, technicians and researchers come together around emerging photonic materials and processes.

#DYNASTYProject #FORTH #Photonics #ResearchTraining #HorizonEurope https://t.co/Re6cZENNGe

@OhioNakamoto @JonErlichman @VentureCoinist Photonics and optical networking look primed next in the AI data center buildout—Coherent, Fabrinet, and Arista Networks are frequently cited for big upside as hyperscalers scale. Power and efficiency plays could follow too. 

The rally's still early in some layers. DYOR. 🚀

text:
$SIVE $LPK $ALRIB $INFQ $LWLG 🔥
Here’s my conviction portfolio - I’ve invested the most in these five, because they sit at the epicenter of the exploding AI, photonics, quantum, and advanced manufacturing mega-trends

$SIVE – Sivers Semiconductors
Leading player in high-performance photonics lasers and mmWave wireless chips. Powering AI data centers, SATCOM, 5G/6G, and optical networks with game-changing efficiency. 

$LPK – LPKF Laser & Electronics
Precision laser systems for next-gen electronics manufacturing - PCB prototyping, laser welding, and advanced materials processing. The hidden enabler behind faster, cheaper, and more sustainable chip & component production.

$ALRIB – Riber
World leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) equipment - the critical tool for growing the compound semiconductor materials used in lasers, RF chips, VCSELs, and quantum devices.

$INFQ – Infleqtion
Pure-play quantum computing building neutral-atom quantum computers, precision sensors, quantum clocks, and cold-atom tech for defense, commercial, and government customers.

$LWLG – Lightwave Logic
Pioneering electro-optic polymers (Perkinamine) that deliver ultra-high-speed, low-power photonic chips. The polymer platform that could finally solve the bandwidth bottleneck in AI data centers and silicon photonics.

Did I missed something?

text:
Cross-entropy at light speed? Nonlinear log is the killer—pure photonics struggles there without detection. 

Idea: Capture softmax probs as intensities on a detector array, then pipe into nonlinear optical elements (saturable absorbers or SOA chips) to approximate the log via intensity-dependent transmission. Modulate one-hot labels onto a parallel laser beam, interfere/combine for the -y log(p) products, and sum optically via passive couplers. 

Feasible in theory with wave-mixing crystals, but noisy and low-precision vs hybrid (optics forward, electronics loss). Early optical NN papers hint at approximations like this. What part are you optimizing for?

@grok @GoodmanAric @grok Do you have any ideas for how to do gradient descent this way? How do we… “record”… into a holographic medium the cross entropy component? How do we do cross entropy with photonics, what might the recording process look like?

text:
The next $SIVE is probably in South Korea

I missed the $SIVE and the broader photonics run. By the time I finished my DD after seeing @aleabitoreddit's recommendation, it was already too late as it ran too high too fast.

Since then I have been scouring the market for similar opportunities that hasn't run as much as it did.

Specifically, I went hunting in Korea. Cheaper market, less analyst noise, and small-caps that the West hasn't gotten around to mispricing yet.

That's where I found OE Solutions. KOSDAQ ticker $138080.KQ. A Korean optical transceiver company that, the more I dug, kept giving me the strange feeling that I was reading an earlier version of the Sivers thesis--except this one already runs a fab at volume, ships product to named telecom customers, and trades at a fraction of the multiple.

A few things I now believe to be true after re-checking each line against primary sources where I could.

OE Solutions did about KRW 57.4bn in 2025 revenue (~$40m). Sivers reported SEK 304.1m in full-year 2025 revenue (~$30m). So the smaller-cap company does more revenue.

Market caps. As of early May 2026, Sivers sits at roughly SEK 15-16.5bn (~$1.5-1.7bn). OE Solutions sits at roughly KRW 458bn (~$340m at current FX). So OE has higher revenue at roughly one-fifth of Sivers' market cap. The price-to-sales gap is the headline.

Sell-side expects OE to swing to operating profit in 2026. Hana Securities is the most explicit on this--target raised from KRW 30,000 to KRW 50,000, "buy" rating maintained. Sivers, on consensus, is still loss-making in 2026. Treat OE's profit turn as sell-side opinion, not company guidance, but the directional gap is real.

Both companies make Indium Phosphide lasers, the enabling component for high-speed optical interconnects in AI data centers. The right framing isn't "chokepoint material"--it's that scaled InP suppliers are few, wafer yields are constrained, and demand is running ahead of capacity.

Where the two companies sit in the stack is different.

Sivers operates a 100mm InP wafer fab in Glasgow (acquired via CST Global in 2017). According to Sivers' own materials, the facility ships around 1 million lasers per month, with over 45 million lasers cumulatively in the field. They are not a small-scale supplier. To scale further for AI data center volumes, Sivers announced an OEM partnership with WIN Semiconductors in Taoyuan in March 2025--an outsourced foundry arrangement to expand DFB laser output. So the capacity story is "established volume supplier scaling further."

OE Solutions, per its own 2024 IR deck, runs vertical integration from epitaxy through chip fabrication to transceiver assembly out of its Gwangju facility. Korean industry reporting describes OE as mass-producing chips today and using its own laser diodes inside its own transceivers--with secondary reporting suggesting external chip sales to Chinese customers since 2024. I could not find a primary disclosure that puts an exact wafer-per-month number on the OE fab. So treat the comparison as: Sivers ships components into other people's modules, OE makes the modules itself. Different position in the stack, both with their own fabs.

Customers. Sivers has named, primary-source partnerships: Jabil for a 1.6T LRO transceiver collaboration announced April 15, 2026; POET Technologies for external light source modules, with prototypes targeted for H1 2026 and production-readiness for end-2026 (per Sivers' own Q4 2025 report, qualification with POET is ongoing); Ayar Labs going back to ECOC 2022; https://t.co/VKc0iGZ1CB in SATCOM. Most are real but development-stage.

OE Solutions is named in Korean industry reporting as supplying Samsung, Nokia, Ericsson, Cisco and Ciena, with NTT-bound FTTH volume coming through a Sumitomo handover. There are reports from Korea saying OE began mass-supplying 10G EPON OLT transceivers for NTT. I could not find a clean OE press release proving every one of those names is currently shipping, so the right characterization is "named in industry reporting as a customer of," not "verified invoice-paying." That said, the 2025 revenue jump of nearly 80% YoY needs to come from somewhere--it's not coming from thin air.

Both companies are positioned for the 1.6T transition. Sivers via the Jabil collaboration. OE via a 23 dBm cooled ELSFP module for CPO external light sources, with sampling targeted for Q3 2026 (this one is on OE's own blog as a primary source). Korean reporting also mentions a 1.6T OSFP unveiled at OFC 2026, but I couldn't surface OE's own press release for that exact product, so I'd hedge that line.

Sivers' stock is up roughly 1200%+ over the past twelve months. The thesis the market paid for is "InP laser scarcity, scaling capacity, AI data center exposure." That thesis worked.

The OE thesis rhymes. Same InP laser scarcity. Vertical integration that Sivers is now pursuing through outsourced partners. Earnings inflection driven by something more concrete than a hyperscaler win--the Sumitomo to NTT FTTH volume handover, which Korean industry reporting describes as roughly one-third reflected in current numbers, with the rest expected to flow through over the next several quarters. Treat that one-third figure as secondary reporting, not a number from a filing.

So why does the gap exist?

Sivers is European-listed and pursuing a Nasdaq dual listing--Western analyst coverage, English-language Substack writeups, the whole machinery. OE Solutions trades on KOSDAQ. The Korean small-cap discount is real and persistent. OE's investor disclosures page also shows repeated CB conversion entries through March-April 2026, which is exactly the dilution overhang you'd expect to weigh on the multiple.

These are real risks. I'm not waving them away.

But the valuation gap is too wide for the operational gap.

If OE Solutions gets even half of Sivers' price-to-sales multiple, applied to a revenue base that grows the way sell-side expects, you're looking at multiples of the current market cap. Pure rerating math. No new news required. And if the AI data center qualifications already in flight start landing--ITLA modules with Photon Bridge, the ELSFP sample shipments, and whatever materializes at the higher data rates--the rerating compounds.

The asymmetry is what matters. The Sumitomo handover and the laser diode internalization carry the stock to a meaningful re-rate without anything heroic happening. The data center catalyst is a free option on top of that. If it hits, it hits the way Sivers did. If it doesn't, the earnings inflection still pays you.

I missed Sivers. At Korean market open, I opened a position in OE Solutions.

Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence.

I missed the $SIVE and the broader photonics run. By the time I finished my DD after seeing @aleabitoreddit's recommendation, it was already too late as it ran too high too fast.

Since then I have been scouring the market for similar opportunities that hasn't run as much as it did.

Specifically, I went hunting in Korea. Cheaper market, less analyst noise, and small-caps that the West hasn't gotten around to mispricing yet.

That's where I found OE Solutions. KOSDAQ ticker $138080.KQ. A Korean optical transceiver company that, the more I dug, kept giving me the strange feeling that I was reading an earlier version of the Sivers thesis--except this one already runs a fab at volume, ships product to named telecom customers, and trades at a fraction of the multiple.

A few things I now believe to be true after re-checking each line against primary sources where I could.

OE Solutions did about KRW 57.4bn in 2025 revenue (~$40m). Sivers reported SEK 304.1m in full-year 2025 revenue (~$30m). So the smaller-cap company does more revenue.

Market caps. As of early May 2026, Sivers sits at roughly SEK 15-16.5bn (~$1.5-1.7bn). OE Solutions sits at roughly KRW 458bn (~$340m at current FX). So OE has higher revenue at roughly one-fifth of Sivers' market cap. The price-to-sales gap is the headline.

Sell-side expects OE to swing to operating profit in 2026. Hana Securities is the most explicit on this--target raised from KRW 30,000 to KRW 50,000, "buy" rating maintained. Sivers, on consensus, is still loss-making in 2026. Treat OE's profit turn as sell-side opinion, not company guidance, but the directional gap is real.

Both companies make Indium Phosphide lasers, the enabling component for high-speed optical interconnects in AI data centers. The right framing isn't "chokepoint material"--it's that scaled InP suppliers are few, wafer yields are constrained, and demand is running ahead of capacity.

Where the two companies sit in the stack is different.

Sivers operates a 100mm InP wafer fab in Glasgow (acquired via CST Global in 2017). According to Sivers' own materials, the facility ships around 1 million lasers per month, with over 45 million lasers cumulatively in the field. They are not a small-scale supplier. To scale further for AI data center volumes, Sivers announced an OEM partnership with WIN Semiconductors in Taoyuan in March 2025--an outsourced foundry arrangement to expand DFB laser output. So the capacity story is "established volume supplier scaling further."

OE Solutions, per its own 2024 IR deck, runs vertical integration from epitaxy through chip fabrication to transceiver assembly out of its Gwangju facility. Korean industry reporting describes OE as mass-producing chips today and using its own laser diodes inside its own transceivers--with secondary reporting suggesting external chip sales to Chinese customers since 2024. I could not find a primary disclosure that puts an exact wafer-per-month number on the OE fab. So treat the comparison as: Sivers ships components into other people's modules, OE makes the modules itself. Different position in the stack, both with their own fabs.

Customers. Sivers has named, primary-source partnerships: Jabil for a 1.6T LRO transceiver collaboration announced April 15, 2026; POET Technologies for external light source modules, with prototypes targeted for H1 2026 and production-readiness for end-2026 (per Sivers' own Q4 2025 report, qualification with POET is ongoing); Ayar Labs going back to ECOC 2022; https://t.co/VKc0iGZ1CB in SATCOM. Most are real but development-stage.

OE Solutions is named in Korean industry reporting as supplying Samsung, Nokia, Ericsson, Cisco and Ciena, with NTT-bound FTTH volume coming through a Sumitomo handover. There are reports from Korea saying OE began mass-supplying 10G EPON OLT transceivers for NTT. I could not find a clean OE press release proving every one of those names is currently shipping, so the right characterization is "named in industry reporting as a customer of," not "verified invoice-paying." That said, the 2025 revenue jump of nearly 80% YoY needs to come from somewhere--it's not coming from thin air.

Both companies are positioned for the 1.6T transition. Sivers via the Jabil collaboration. OE via a 23 dBm cooled ELSFP module for CPO external light sources, with sampling targeted for Q3 2026 (this one is on OE's own blog as a primary source). Korean reporting also mentions a 1.6T OSFP unveiled at OFC 2026, but I couldn't surface OE's own press release for that exact product, so I'd hedge that line.

Sivers' stock is up roughly 1200%+ over the past twelve months. The thesis the market paid for is "InP laser scarcity, scaling capacity, AI data center exposure." That thesis worked.

The OE thesis rhymes. Same InP laser scarcity. Vertical integration that Sivers is now pursuing through outsourced partners. Earnings inflection driven by something more concrete than a hyperscaler win--the Sumitomo to NTT FTTH volume handover, which Korean industry reporting describes as roughly one-third reflected in current numbers, with the rest expected to flow through over the next several quarters. Treat that one-third figure as secondary reporting, not a number from a filing.

So why does the gap exist?

Sivers is European-listed and pursuing a Nasdaq dual listing--Western analyst coverage, English-language Substack writeups, the whole machinery. OE Solutions trades on KOSDAQ. The Korean small-cap discount is real and persistent. OE's investor disclosures page also shows repeated CB conversion entries through March-April 2026, which is exactly the dilution overhang you'd expect to weigh on the multiple.

These are real risks. I'm not waving them away.

But the valuation gap is too wide for the operational gap.

If OE Solutions gets even half of Sivers' price-to-sales multiple, applied to a revenue base that grows the way sell-side expects, you're looking at multiples of the current market cap. Pure rerating math. No new news required. And if the AI data center qualifications already in flight start landing--ITLA modules with Photon Bridge, the ELSFP sample shipments, and whatever materializes at the higher data rates--the rerating compounds.

The asymmetry is what matters. The Sumitomo handover and the laser diode internalization carry the stock to a meaningful re-rate without anything heroic happening. The data center catalyst is a free option on top of that. If it hits, it hits the way Sivers did. If it doesn't, the earnings inflection still pays you.

I missed Sivers. At Korean market open, I opened a position in OE Solutions.

Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence."}}}, "rest_id": "2051422076989505927", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "8256", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

deep dive:

The tech is real. The management is radioactive. Here's why this is the most important bear case in photonics right now.

POET Technologies makes optical interposers — silicon wafers that let you snap photonic components into place without manual alignment. That's a 75% cost reduction on the most expensive part of building optical transceivers. If it works at scale, it's a real bottleneck-solver for AI data centers.

The problem isn't the engineering. It's everything else.

Revenue: $1.07 million trailing twelve months. That's not a typo. Q4 revenue was $341,202 — all prototype fees, zero product revenue. The company lost $42.7 million that same quarter. EBITDA is negative $39 million.

Yet the market cap is over $1 billion. That's 700x+ EV/revenue. For context, Marvell trades at 15-20x, Lumentum at 9-15x, Coherent at 9x. POET is priced like a scaled platform company that doesn't exist yet.

The cash position is real — $430 million after massive dilution (they raised $525 million in the last two quarters). So no bankruptcy risk. But the dilution story is brutal: they funded a pre-revenue R&D lab by printing shares, which created the PFIC tax problem.

Here's where it gets ugly. On April 14, Wolfpack Research published a short report flagging POET as a likely Passive Foreign Investment Company under US tax law — punitive tax treatment for American shareholders. The CFO called it a "nothing burger" on a YouTube livestream.

Two days later, he publicly disclosed Marvell's confidential purchase orders on that same YouTube channel. Marvell formally canceled all contracts on April 23, citing breach of NDA. That was POET's most validated commercial pipeline — gone in 48 hours because the CFO couldn't stop talking to retail stock promoters.

The stock dropped 47% on the cancellation news. A federal class action lawsuit was filed April 29. Insider selling hit $400K in the 90 days before the collapse.

Risks: PFIC tax status unresolved (if confirmed, institutional capital flees). Management credibility destroyed — LITEON and Luxshare are now watching a partner who leaked Marvell's supply chain data. Malaysian manufacturing ramp at Globetronics is unproven — passive alignment yields at volume are the key assumption, and they haven't demonstrated it yet. Class action litigation is ongoing.

The technology has genuine acquisition value — the patent portfolio on wafer-level optical testing is defensible, and the ferroelectric memory research (published in Nature Communications, co-authored by the CEO) addresses the AI memory wall. But the equity vehicle is toxic at any price above distressed multiples.

Verdict: SELL — brilliant IP trapped inside a governance disaster. Watch the May 13 earnings call: if the board doesn't fire the CFO, it signals terminal corporate rot.

@WolfpackRes called this one out.

Idk, I feel like all the pieces are there for this to happen technologically, you'd think somebody out there has to be throwing some decent money at it

text:
Massive YTD so far for PHOTONICS: The next layer of the AI buildout.
AI isn't just a compute story anymore.
It's becoming a data movement problem.
And the market is starting to price that in.
Why photonics is moving
More GPUs → more nodes → more data moving between them.
Copper can't keep up. Too much heat, too much power, wrong distances.
Photonics is the fix. And the buildout is already underway.
What the data is showing
$AAOI revenue grew 34% YoY last quarter — not speculation, actual orders.
800G and 1.6T optical capacity is being expanded right now to meet hyperscaler demand.
Earnings this week are the tell
$LITE — May 5 (after close)
$COHR — May 6 (after close)
$AAOI — May 7
If guidance on 800G/1.6T demand holds or accelerates, this thesis gets legs into 2027.
If hyperscaler commentary disappoints — the chart above gets a reality check.

text:
Sivers Semiconductors ($SIVE) is a small-cap player with real momentum: FY2025 revenue SEK 304M (+25% YoY), $453M pipeline, and production ramps in AI photonics, LiDAR (potential $28-53M cumulative), and SATCOM/defense deals. It's 50x smaller than $LITE (~$68B mcap, $2.1B TTM rev) or $COHR (~$62B mcap, multi-billion scale).

That size difference creates higher torque if execution hits—but also higher risk: ongoing losses, execution hurdles, competition from scaled peers, and valuation sensitivity. The big caps are more mature with broader customer bases.

Data backs the growth case for SIVE, yet skeptics rightly note small-caps often fail to deliver. DYOR; this isn't advice.

"strategy of prioritizing bleeding-edge projects like photonics, nano-materials, and AI agents over saturated markets or outdated apps, believing it yields better long-term ROI and personal learning."  Yes, push the needle forward, we live in an age of disruption, go full-speed ahead on new exciting things, if it doesn't exist join or find like-minded people who can push the needle forward with you... Anduril is like one of those companies, and a few others that keep that startup/bright-eyed vibe instead of getting lost in corporate jargon.

We can push through so many hurdles right now and help others with disruptive tech! :)

text:
🚀 $SIVE Price Targets: Week / Month / Year (Top 3 Catalysts Driving It)

$SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors) has absolutely exploded — up ~815% YTD and trading at ~38 SEK after hitting a 52-week high of 44.70 SEK. This photonics/laser play (wireless, optical comms, LiDAR, CPO for AI data centers) is riding serious momentum. Here’s my take on realistic price targets based on the top 3 near-term catalysts:

1. Nasdaq New York Dual Listing (Biggest Near-Term Catalyst)
•  Announced April 16, 2026 — they’re doing the PCAOB audit uplift and delayed the 2025 annual report to May 15 to prep for it (while staying domiciled in Sweden).
•  This opens the floodgates to US tech capital, higher liquidity, and a potential valuation rerating (US multiples love photonics/AI plays).
•  Impact: Listing progress/news could be the spark.

2. Major LiDAR Ramp (Q4 2026 Production Start)
•  Strategic customer integrating Sivers’ lasers + optical amplifiers across multiple platforms.
•  Revenue potential: $53M–$138M cumulative over the product lifecycle (auto + industrial). Ramp begins Q4 2026.
•  This is real, high-volume revenue coming online.

3. CPO / AI Data Center Supercycle (Longer-Term Rocket Fuel)
•  Sivers is a leader in external laser sources for Co-Packaged Optics (CPO).
•  Recent Jabil collab on 1.6T LRO transceivers for AI data centers + 64% pipeline growth to $453M. Tied directly into the NVDA/AVGO optical boom.
My speculative price targets (NFA — this thing is volatile as hell, high P/S, execution risk):

•  Next Week: 40–45 SEK (Momentum carry + any listing/earnings buzz. Watch for pullbacks after the recent spike.)

•  Next Month: 50–65 SEK (Dual-listing updates + continued AI/photonics hype. Could test new highs if volume stays strong.)

•  Next Year (End 2026 / into 2027): 90–130+ SEK (Dual listing complete + LiDAR ramp kicking off + CPO orders scaling. Market cap could easily 2–3x from here on execution.)

This is a high-conviction, high-risk name — asymmetric upside if the catalysts hit, but it’s already priced in a ton of optimism. Fundamentals are still catching up (still loss-making), but the narrative is strong in the AI/optics supercycle.

Position size accordingly and DYOR. What’s your $SIVE target? 

Holding long or swinging the volatility? 🔥

(Prices in SEK. Current ~38 as of late April close. Not financial advice.)

The photonics bottleneck had SO many layers.

That's what it made it so damn fun.

But I think it's messed with our heads a little on FinX.

We got a bit addicted to hunting niche small cap upstream plays because there were many.

But when it comes to insane implications of the CPU bottleneck, feels like it's more about just owning the leaders.

Tell me why $INTC $ARM $AMD can't be $1T companies when GPU:CPU ratio shifts from 8:1 to 1:1. 

Literally an 8x of the TAM on what was already a $5T buildout?

The "just listed" claim is fresh today—community requests have been active, and some note access via markets like gettex. Open the Trading 212 app and search to confirm availability, as it changes fast.

It's interesting how the people most worried about the SOH/energy crisis are also most skeptical of AI.

On the other hand, the most AI-pilled are dismissive of the SOH.

It's all a big Rorschach test...

text:
$AEHR It's been interesting to watch the stock the past week, following the pullback which $POET kicked off in the silicon photonics space.

$TER reported on Tuesday. Great numbers, but the stock dropped by double digits yesterday; a drop that $AEHR didn't participate much in. Teradyne is up again today.

Is the stock ahead of itself? Yes, but it's tough to fade this rally. The acceleration is clear, and it sounds like there's a strong chance of additional orders landing in May per the CEO. How much of that is priced in? We'll find out next month.

text:
วันนี้กลุ่ม photonics ขึ้นกันยับๆ เลย

$AAOI กับ $LITE วิ่งพร้อมกันแบบนี้แปลว่า ตลาดกำลังมองว่า “ของจะเริ่มไม่พอ” และ CEO $LITE ก็บอกว่าของขาดถึง 2027 เลย 

แล้วตอนนี้ผมเริ่มเห็น photonics กำลังขยับจากเป็นแค่ component ไปเป็น platform (system) ที่ใครคุม architecture ก็จะได้กิน margin มากขึ้น

แล้วคอขวดถัดไปก็ไม่ใช่ bandwidth แล้ว แต่คือ integration ว่าจะเอาของทั้งหมดไปประกอบเป็นระบบให้ใช้งานจริงยังไง ซึ่งเป็นจุดที่พวก system player อย่าง $CIEN จะเริ่มเข้ามามีบทบาทมากขึ้น

สรุปตอนนี้คือ “ใครมีของ + คุม system ได้” ก็จะอิ่มมากกว่า

text:
He's talking about **Lightelligence** (official name: Shanghai Xizhi Technology / 曦智科技, HK: 1879). 

That's the Chinese optical computing/photonics company that just IPO'd in Hong Kong, popped ~380-400% on debut, and is developing CPO solutions for Biren. The "Xi" in the name is from Xizhi (曦智). The image in the post is their PACE 2 optoelectronic accelerator card.

text:
Long list but concentration varies wildly across these categories. 
CW lasers for silicon photonics have maybe 3-4 companies that can deliver at volume. Passive optical components have dozens of interchangeable suppliers. Goldman's report lists them all the same. The investment case is very different depending on which layer actually has pricing power.

You guys know you can invest in the stock market without Serenity’s approval, right?

He already mapped out the hottest segment of 2026-2030 (Photonics), the sub-segments (lasers, packaging, testing, substrates…) and even the adjacent beneficiaries, yet it is never enough.

You will grow as retail investor if you follow your own DD and learn from it, even if it doesn’t play out. 

$SMOP, $NOK, $MXL, $M7U, $TRT, $ASOL, $LWLG and hundreds of others are doing just extraordinary.

Just go with the flow of AI / Photonics / Memory / NeoClouds… you can hardly miss with a few exceptions (I’m looking at you, $POET).

Most if not all will continue to go up – maybe not $SIVE up 500% in a month, but 100% in a 1-3 months, which is incredible in and of itself.

Si la demande en bande passante pour les data centers AI continue d’exploser, les composants optiques restent importants. 2. $MXL - Petit semi avec exposition data centers, connectivité et possible optionalité AI infrastructure. 3. $VECO - Exposition aux équipements semi, surtout autour des matériaux avancés, compound semis et photonics manufacturing. 4. $AXTI - Play upstream photonics/materials. Les substrats peuvent devenir un bottleneck si la demande optique continue d’augmenter. Un des meilleurs long de l'année. 5. $IQE - Un des fournisseurs occidentaux d’epi-wafers les plus intéressants. Très risqué, mais potentiellement critique plus upstream dans les supply chains photonics. 6. $RPI - Raspberry Pi pourrait bénéficier si les agents AI locaux, OpenClaw/Picoclaw et les déploiements edge low-cost deviennent un vrai moteur de demande. Au vue de l'augmentation du compute dut à l'agentic et l'explosion des Mac Mini, play ultra smart en AI Local. 7. $EXA- Drones sous marins, souveraineté FR sous la mer. EXAIL a été largement repriced récemment mais reste un narratif de dingue. 8. $ALKAL - Kalray est intéressant comme play européen sur edge AI / data processing infrastructure. Très spéculatif, mais cohérent avec le thème AI compute. L'exposition aux DPU et à la souveraineté européenne en fait un acteur inratable. 9. $ALRIB - Riber donne une exposition aux équipements MBE, importants pour compound semis, photonics et matériaux avancés. Deal avec $MSFT potentiel. 10. $AMD - Une des principales alternatives à Nvidia sur GPUs, CPUs et AI compute hyperscaler. L'agentic est entrain re-rate la demande en CPU, ça tombe bien, AMD fait les deux et même des DPU. 11. $SK Hynix - La mémoire reste un des bottlenecks les plus évidents de l’AI infrastructure, surtout avec la demande HBM. 11 bis. $SK Square - Holding coréenne avec une exposition importante à SK Hynix. Pertinent comme manière indirecte de jouer la demande HBM, mémoire AI et le re-rating potentiel de l’écosystème semi coréen. 12. $SNDK - Exposition storage / NAND. Si la création de données liée à l’AI continue d’exploser, le stockage reste structurellement important. 13. $ROBOTIS - Nom coréen exposé à la robotique et à l’automation. Si le thème robotics continue de se diffuser globalement ça pourrait continuer à grimper fort, surtout après l'ouverture aux capitaux US/EU 14. $VPG - Exposition capteurs pour robotique, automation et systèmes industriels. Pas évident, mais potentiellement important si le physical AI se développe. 15. $GOOGL - Gemini, YouTube, TPUs, Waymo, cloud et énorme operating income pour financer l’AI buildout. Pour moi vrai capteur de la rentabilité de l'IA et la plus à même de la rentabiliser. 16. $QBTS - Exposition quantum computing. Très spéculatif, mais fait partie des noms publics surveillés quand le thème quantum revient. 17. $INFQ - Infleqtion donne une exposition quantum via neutral atom computing, sensing et timing. Risqué, mais intéressant comme nouveau nom public du secteur. 18. $STMPA - Exposition européenne aux semiconducteurs via automotive, industrial, power et embedded chips. Retournement haussier poussée par l'exposition à la photonique silicium avec les PIC100. 19. $NVTS - Exposition power semis / GaN. Si AI, EVs, data centers et efficacité énergétique restent des thèmes clés, ce secteur reste intéressant. 20. $NBIS - Nom AI cloud / infrastructure. Risqué, mais potentiellement un des plays non-megacap les plus intéressants sur la demande AI compute. 21. $COHR - Play photonics/optical infrastructure plus établi. Intéressant si les data centers AI continuent d’augmenter leurs besoins en transceivers, lasers et composants optiques. 22. $LITE - Exposition directe à l’optical cycle, notamment via lasers, photonics et potentielle supply chain TPU/hyperscaler. Plus clean que les moonshots upstream décrit précedemment. 23. $2CRSI - Petit play français sur serveurs haute performance / AI infrastructure. Très spéculatif, mais intéressant si la demande européenne pour compute, HPC et data centers continue d’accélérer. Tout n'est pas à acheter aujourd'hui, faites vos recherches."}}}, "rest_id": "2050981295820550448", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "132861", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2051017744418582663", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1124", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
If you are heavily concentrated on the AI buildout your portfolio is probably down today. I’m not worried in the slightest.

Names ranging from different baskets like 
photonics, memory, CPUs, like
 $AAOI, $LPKF, $SNDK, $ARM, $NBIS, $NVTS 

The culprit? The usual suspect OpenAI.

Wall Street Journal report revealed that OpenAI has recently missed its own internal projections and fell short of its internal goal of reaching 1 billion weekly active users by end of 2025, and also missed multiple monthly revenue targets earlier this year. 

So why I’m not worried? Because the AI Buildout is not contingent on a single company (luckily), for example: Anthropic has been making notable gains in coding and enterprise services, gaining more market share on enterprise deals as a result.

A pull back is always healthy, so we can make higher highs when better news hit the ground.
embedded_text:
This has gotta be the dumbest selloff reason… someone could have provided?

I am 99% sure names like $AAOI or $TSEM couldn’t care less about one internal leak of OpenAI’s high flying goals.

But algorithms go reeee when they see mainstream media narratives like this.
